agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring a logistics platform from shipment-level tracking to parcel-level tracking to enable partial delivery detection. Currently the system treats a shipment as a single atomic unit with one tracking number and one status. The business requires individual parcel lifecycle management so that scenarios like partial delivery, individual parcel loss, and customer disputes on partial receipt can be properly handled.","bounded_context":"Shipment & Parcel Tracking — the subdomain responsible for tracking the physical movement and delivery status of goods from origin to destination. This context owns the lifecycle of shipments and their constituent parcels, and emits domain events consumed by downstream contexts (Billing, Customer Service, Claims).","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A logical grouping of one or more parcels originating from the same sender to the same recipient under a single order or consignment. A shipment is the unit of customer intent — what the customer ordered shipped."},{"term":"Parcel","definition":"A physically distinct, individually trackable package within a shipment. Each parcel has its own tracking number, weight, dimensions, and independent lifecycle. The parcel is the unit of physical fulfillment."},{"term":"Parcel Status","definition":"The current state of an individual parcel in its lifecycle: Created, In Transit, Out for Delivery, Delivered, Failed Delivery Attempt, Lost, Returned, Held."},{"term":"Shipment Status","definition":"A derived (computed) status reflecting the aggregate state of all parcels within the shipment. Not independently set — always calculated from parcel statuses."},{"term":"Partial Delivery","definition":"A shipment state where at least one but not all parcels have been delivered. This is a first-class business concept that triggers specific customer communication and billing logic."},{"term":"Tracking Number","definition":"A unique identifier assigned to each parcel (not to the shipment) that enables external tracking by carriers and customers. The shipment retains a consignment number for grouping."},{"term":"Consignment Number","definition":"A unique identifier for the shipment as a whole, used to group parcels and reference the customer's original order."},{"term":"Delivery Confirmation","definition":"Proof that a specific parcel was delivered — timestamp, GPS coordinates, signature, or photo. Captured per-parcel, not per-shipment."},{"term":"Parcel Exception","definition":"Any event that disrupts the normal parcel lifecycle — failed delivery attempt, damage, loss, held at customs. Each exception is parcel-specific."},{"term":"Dispute","definition":"A customer-initiated claim that the actual delivery state of one or more parcels does not match what the system records. Must reference specific parcels."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every parcel within a shipment MUST have its own unique tracking number and independent status.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the core data integrity requirement of the refactor. Without independent tracking numbers and statuses, parcel-level tracking is impossible and the system is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment with N parcels, the system stores exactly N distinct tracking numbers and N independently updatable parcel statuses."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Shipment status MUST be derived (computed) from the aggregate of its parcel statuses, never set independently.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If shipment status can be set independently of parcel statuses, the system can represent contradictory states (e.g., shipment marked Delivered while parcels are In Transit), corrupting business reporting and customer trust.","testable_assertion":"There is no API or internal method that directly sets shipment status. Shipment status is always recalculated when any parcel status changes."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A shipment is considered Delivered only when ALL of its parcels have status Delivered.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Marking a shipment as Delivered when parcels are outstanding would misrepresent fulfillment to the customer and downstream systems (billing, SLA tracking). This is a physical constraint — you cannot have delivered a shipment if packages are still missing.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment with 5 parcels where 4 are Delivered and 1 is In Transit, the shipment status is Partially Delivered, not Delivered."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A shipment enters Partially Delivered status when at least one parcel is Delivered AND at least one parcel is not Delivered (and not Lost/Returned).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Partial delivery is a distinct business state that triggers specific customer notifications, billing adjustments, and SLA calculations. Conflating it with other states corrupts downstream business logic.","testable_assertion":"Given 3 of 5 parcels Delivered and 2 In Transit, shipment status equals Partially Delivered."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A parcel marked as Lost MUST trigger a Parcel Exception event and initiate the claims workflow for that specific parcel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Lost parcels represent financial liability and legal obligation. Failing to trigger the claims workflow means the company silently absorbs loss without recourse or customer remedy, corrupting financial integrity.","testable_assertion":"When a parcel transitions to Lost status, a ParcelLostEvent is emitted containing the parcel tracking number, shipment consignment number, and timestamp."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Parcel status transitions must follow the defined state machine — no skipping states except for exception paths (e.g., Created → Lost is valid for parcels lost in warehouse).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Arbitrary state transitions corrupt audit trails and make it impossible to reason about where in the physical process a parcel is. The state machine encodes physical reality.","testable_assertion":"Attempting to transition a parcel from Delivered to In Transit throws an InvalidStateTransitionException."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A customer dispute MUST reference specific parcel tracking numbers, not just the shipment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Disputes that reference only the shipment are unresolvable when parcels have independent lifecycles. The investigation, proof of delivery, and resolution must be parcel-specific to be actionable.","testable_assertion":"Creating a dispute without at least one parcel tracking number is rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"A shipment with ALL parcels in terminal states (Delivered, Lost, Returned) is considered Closed. A shipment with any parcel in a non-terminal state remains Active.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The definition of terminal states and the Closed concept is a business policy. Management could decide to add new terminal states or change when a shipment is considered resolved.","testable_assertion":"Given all 5 parcels with statuses [Delivered, Delivered, Lost, Returned, Delivered], shipment status is Closed."},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"When a parcel status changes, the system MUST persist a timestamped audit log entry for that parcel including old status, new status, actor, and reason.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Audit trail integrity is essential for dispute resolution, regulatory compliance, and carrier accountability. Without per-parcel audit logs, partial delivery disputes are unresolvable.","testable_assertion":"After 3 status transitions on a parcel, the audit log contains exactly 3 entries with correct old/new status pairs and timestamps."},{"id":"BR-10","rule":"Removing a parcel from a shipment after the shipment has left Created status is not permitted. Parcels can only be added or removed while the shipment is being assembled.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Once parcels are physically in transit, removing them from the data model creates orphaned physical packages and breaks tracking integrity. This is a physical constraint.","testable_assertion":"Attempting to remove a parcel from a shipment where any parcel has status beyond Created throws a ShipmentAlreadyDispatchedException."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A shipment is created with 5 parcels","when":"The shipment is persisted","then":"Each of the 5 parcels has a unique tracking number, status of Created, and the shipment has a consignment number distinct from any parcel tracking number."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A shipment with 5 parcels where 3 are Delivered and 2 are In Transit","when":"The shipment status is queried","then":"The shipment status is Partially Delivered and the response includes individual status for each parcel."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A shipment with 5 parcels where all 5 are Delivered","when":"The last parcel transitions to Delivered","then":"The shipment status automatically transitions to Delivered and a ShipmentDeliveredEvent is emitted."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A shipment with 5 parcels where 1 parcel is reported lost","when":"The parcel transitions to Lost","then":"A ParcelLostEvent is emitted, the shipment status reflects the loss (e.g., Partially Delivered if others are delivered, or In Transit with Exception), and the claims workflow is initiated for that parcel only."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A customer received 3 of 5 parcels but claims they only received 2","when":"The customer opens a dispute","then":"The dispute references the specific parcel tracking number(s) in question, and the system retrieves per-parcel delivery confirmation (signature, photo, GPS) for investigation."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A parcel has status Delivered with delivery confirmation","when":"An attempt is made to transition it to In Transit","then":"The transition is rejected with an InvalidStateTransitionException and the parcel remains Delivered."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A shipment with parcels where some are Delivered and one is Lost","when":"All non-delivered, non-lost parcels reach a terminal state","then":"The shipment transitions to Closed and a ShipmentClosedEvent is emitted summarizing the final state of each parcel."},{"id":"AC-8","given":"The legacy system has shipments with a single tracking number and single status","when":"The migration runs","then":"Each existing shipment is converted to have one parcel (1:1) preserving the original tracking number, and no data is lost. The shipment gains a new consignment number."},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A parcel transitions from In Transit to Out for Delivery","when":"The transition is recorded","then":"An audit log entry is created with the parcel tracking number, old status, new status, timestamp, and actor (carrier/system)."},{"id":"AC-10","given":"A shipment is in Created status with 3 parcels","when":"A parcel is removed before dispatch","then":"The parcel is removed, the shipment now has 2 parcels, and the operation succeeds."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"All parcels in a shipment are Lost","expected_behavior":"Shipment status becomes All Lost (or a Closed variant). A ShipmentLostEvent is emitted. The entire shipment enters the claims workflow, not just individual parcels."},{"scenario":"A parcel is delivered but then the customer disputes receipt, and investigation confirms non-delivery","expected_behavior":"The parcel status must support a reversal path: Delivered → Disputed → Under Investigation → resolution (Confirmed Delivered or Delivery Revoked). If revoked, the parcel re-enters the fulfillment or refund process."},{"scenario":"Carrier provides a status update for a tracking number that doesn't match any parcel in the system","expected_behavior":"The update is logged to a dead-letter queue for manual investigation. No parcel status is changed. An alert is raised to operations."},{"scenario":"Two parcels in the same shipment are with different carriers","expected_behavior":"Each parcel tracks its carrier independently. Shipment status aggregation works regardless of carrier. Carrier-specific tracking events are normalized to the common parcel status model."},{"scenario":"Customer refuses delivery of some parcels but accepts others","expected_behavior":"Refused parcels transition to Returned. Accepted parcels transition to Delivered. Shipment status reflects the mixed state (Partially Delivered) until returned parcels reach their terminal state."},{"scenario":"A parcel is held at customs indefinitely","expected_behavior":"Parcel transitions to Held status. The shipment remains Active. If the hold exceeds a configurable threshold, an alert is raised and the customer is notified about the specific parcel."},{"scenario":"Single-parcel shipment (backward compatibility with legacy data)","expected_behavior":"The system behaves identically to multi-parcel shipments. A single-parcel shipment's derived status always matches its sole parcel's status. No special-casing in the domain model."},{"scenario":"Concurrent status updates arrive for multiple parcels in the same shipment","expected_behavior":"Each parcel status update is processed independently. Shipment status derivation is recalculated after each parcel update. Optimistic locking or event ordering prevents shipment-level race conditions."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Parcel-level tracking enables accurate partial delivery billing — the company can invoice for delivered parcels without waiting for the entire shipment. It also reduces dispute resolution costs by providing per-parcel proof of delivery, and limits loss liability to individual parcels rather than entire shipments.","operational":"Operations teams gain granular visibility into fulfillment performance. They can identify which carrier legs or warehouses are causing parcel-level failures. Exception handling becomes targeted (one parcel, not entire shipment). However, the migration from single-unit to multi-parcel tracking is a significant operational risk that requires careful data migration and a rollback plan.","user":"Customers gain transparency into exactly which parcels have arrived and which are pending. This reduces support contacts ('where is my stuff?') and increases trust. Partial delivery visibility sets correct expectations rather than showing a misleading single status for a complex shipment.","risk":"Data migration from legacy single-tracking to multi-parcel model is the highest risk. Incorrect migration could orphan tracking data or create phantom parcels. The state machine complexity increases significantly — more states, more transitions, more edge cases. Carrier integration must be updated to map carrier events to individual parcels rather than shipments.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Gain per-parcel visibility and can file targeted disputes. Must adapt to new tracking UX showing multiple tracking numbers per order."},{"group":"Customer Service","impact":"Dispute resolution becomes more precise but also more complex. Agents need training on parcel-level investigation workflows."},{"group":"Operations / Warehouse","impact":"Must assign individual tracking numbers at the parcel level during packing. This changes the packing workflow and requires label-per-parcel capability."},{"group":"Carrier Partners","impact":"Integration contracts may need updating if carriers currently provide shipment-level updates. Need to ensure carrier tracking events can be mapped to individual parcels."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Can now support partial delivery invoicing and parcel-level insurance claims. Billing logic must be updated to handle partial fulfillment."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Significant refactor of the core domain model. Must handle backward compatibility, data migration, and increased state machine complexity."}]},"confidence":{"level":82,"reasoning":"The business requirement is clearly stated and well-understood in the logistics domain. Parcel-level tracking with partial delivery detection is a standard industry pattern. The domain rules and state machine are well-defined by physical logistics reality. Confidence is not higher because: (1) the specific parcel status state machine may need refinement based on the actual carrier integrations in use, (2) the dispute resolution workflow details depend on existing claims processes not described, and (3) the migration strategy from legacy data needs careful planning with engineering.","high_confidence_areas":["Shipment-Parcel entity relationship (1:N composition)","Shipment status must be derived from parcel statuses","Each parcel needs independent tracking number and lifecycle","Partial delivery is a first-class business state","Disputes must be parcel-specific","Audit trail per parcel is mandatory"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact set of parcel statuses — Held, Failed Delivery Attempt, and other exception states depend on which carriers are integrated and what events they emit","Dispute resolution workflow details — how does investigation proceed, what are SLAs, who arbitrates?","Whether consignment number is a new concept or maps to an existing identifier in the current system","Billing model for partial delivery — is it per-parcel billing, or still per-shipment with adjustments?","Regulatory or compliance requirements that may constrain status retention periods or audit requirements"],"assumptions":["The current system has a 1:1 relationship between shipment and tracking number that will become 1:N (shipment:parcels)","Carriers can provide parcel-level tracking events (not just shipment-level)","The business wants real-time status derivation for shipments, not batch recalculation","A parcel belongs to exactly one shipment and cannot be moved between shipments","The migration will use a 1:1 conversion for existing shipments (each legacy shipment becomes a shipment with one parcel)"]},"escalations":[],"rejection_reasons":[]}

trace.result: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring a logistics platform from shipment-level tracking to parcel-level tracking to enable partial delivery detection. Currently the system treats a shipment as a single atomic unit with one tracking number and one status. The business requires individual parcel lifecycle management so that scenarios like partial delivery, individual parcel loss, and customer disputes on partial receipt can be properly handled.","bounded_context":"Shipment & Parcel Tracking — the subdomain responsible for tracking the physical movement and delivery status of goods from origin to destination. This context owns the lifecycle of shipments and their constituent parcels, and emits domain events consumed by downstream contexts (Billing, Customer Service, Claims).","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A logical grouping of one or more parcels originating from the same sender to the same recipient under a single order or consignment. A shipment is the unit of customer intent — what the customer ordered shipped."},{"term":"Parcel","definition":"A physically distinct, individually trackable package within a shipment. Each parcel has its own tracking number, weight, dimensions, and independent lifecycle. The parcel is the unit of physical fulfillment."},{"term":"Parcel Status","definition":"The current state of an individual parcel in its lifecycle: Created, In Transit, Out for Delivery, Delivered, Failed Delivery Attempt, Lost, Returned, Held."},{"term":"Shipment Status","definition":"A derived (computed) status reflecting the aggregate state of all parcels within the shipment. Not independently set — always calculated from parcel statuses."},{"term":"Partial Delivery","definition":"A shipment state where at least one but not all parcels have been delivered. This is a first-class business concept that triggers specific customer communication and billing logic."},{"term":"Tracking Number","definition":"A unique identifier assigned to each parcel (not to the shipment) that enables external tracking by carriers and customers. The shipment retains a consignment number for grouping."},{"term":"Consignment Number","definition":"A unique identifier for the shipment as a whole, used to group parcels and reference the customer's original order."},{"term":"Delivery Confirmation","definition":"Proof that a specific parcel was delivered — timestamp, GPS coordinates, signature, or photo. Captured per-parcel, not per-shipment."},{"term":"Parcel Exception","definition":"Any event that disrupts the normal parcel lifecycle — failed delivery attempt, damage, loss, held at customs. Each exception is parcel-specific."},{"term":"Dispute","definition":"A customer-initiated claim that the actual delivery state of one or more parcels does not match what the system records. Must reference specific parcels."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every parcel within a shipment MUST have its own unique tracking number and independent status.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the core data integrity requirement of the refactor. Without independent tracking numbers and statuses, parcel-level tracking is impossible and the system is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment with N parcels, the system stores exactly N distinct tracking numbers and N independently updatable parcel statuses."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Shipment status MUST be derived (computed) from the aggregate of its parcel statuses, never set independently.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If shipment status can be set independently of parcel statuses, the system can represent contradictory states (e.g., shipment marked Delivered while parcels are In Transit), corrupting business reporting and customer trust.","testable_assertion":"There is no API or internal method that directly sets shipment status. Shipment status is always recalculated when any parcel status changes."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A shipment is considered Delivered only when ALL of its parcels have status Delivered.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Marking a shipment as Delivered when parcels are outstanding would misrepresent fulfillment to the customer and downstream systems (billing, SLA tracking). This is a physical constraint — you cannot have delivered a shipment if packages are still missing.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment with 5 parcels where 4 are Delivered and 1 is In Transit, the shipment status is Partially Delivered, not Delivered."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A shipment enters Partially Delivered status when at least one parcel is Delivered AND at least one parcel is not Delivered (and not Lost/Returned).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Partial delivery is a distinct business state that triggers specific customer notifications, billing adjustments, and SLA calculations. Conflating it with other states corrupts downstream business logic.","testable_assertion":"Given 3 of 5 parcels Delivered and 2 In Transit, shipment status equals Partially Delivered."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A parcel marked as Lost MUST trigger a Parcel Exception event and initiate the claims workflow for that specific parcel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Lost parcels represent financial liability and legal obligation. Failing to trigger the claims workflow means the company silently absorbs loss without recourse or customer remedy, corrupting financial integrity.","testable_assertion":"When a parcel transitions to Lost status, a ParcelLostEvent is emitted containing the parcel tracking number, shipment consignment number, and timestamp."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Parcel status transitions must follow the defined state machine — no skipping states except for exception paths (e.g., Created → Lost is valid for parcels lost in warehouse).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Arbitrary state transitions corrupt audit trails and make it impossible to reason about where in the physical process a parcel is. The state machine encodes physical reality.","testable_assertion":"Attempting to transition a parcel from Delivered to In Transit throws an InvalidStateTransitionException."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A customer dispute MUST reference specific parcel tracking numbers, not just the shipment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Disputes that reference only the shipment are unresolvable when parcels have independent lifecycles. The investigation, proof of delivery, and resolution must be parcel-specific to be actionable.","testable_assertion":"Creating a dispute without at least one parcel tracking number is rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"A shipment with ALL parcels in terminal states (Delivered, Lost, Returned) is considered Closed. A shipment with any parcel in a non-terminal state remains Active.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The definition of terminal states and the Closed concept is a business policy. Management could decide to add new terminal states or change when a shipment is considered resolved.","testable_assertion":"Given all 5 parcels with statuses [Delivered, Delivered, Lost, Returned, Delivered], shipment status is Closed."},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"When a parcel status changes, the system MUST persist a timestamped audit log entry for that parcel including old status, new status, actor, and reason.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Audit trail integrity is essential for dispute resolution, regulatory compliance, and carrier accountability. Without per-parcel audit logs, partial delivery disputes are unresolvable.","testable_assertion":"After 3 status transitions on a parcel, the audit log contains exactly 3 entries with correct old/new status pairs and timestamps."},{"id":"BR-10","rule":"Removing a parcel from a shipment after the shipment has left Created status is not permitted. Parcels can only be added or removed while the shipment is being assembled.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Once parcels are physically in transit, removing them from the data model creates orphaned physical packages and breaks tracking integrity. This is a physical constraint.","testable_assertion":"Attempting to remove a parcel from a shipment where any parcel has status beyond Created throws a ShipmentAlreadyDispatchedException."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A shipment is created with 5 parcels","when":"The shipment is persisted","then":"Each of the 5 parcels has a unique It also reduces dispute resolution costs by providing per-parcel proof of delivery, and limits loss liability to individual parcels rather than entire shipments.","operational":"Operations teams gain granular visibility into fulfillment performance.
